title: Toy Sink With Running Water
summary: A working toy kitchen sink with a hand-pump-driven faucet, built from wood around a plastic basin.
description:
Most toy kitchen sinks are purely decorative — a molded plastic bowl with a fixed faucet that does nothing. This one actually runs water, using a small submersible pump hidden inside the cabinet to push water up through a real faucet assembly built from plumbing parts.

**Choose the plastic basin first, then build the wood cabinet around it.** Every other dimension in this project derives from the container's footprint, so there's no fixed cut list to follow — measure your own basin before cutting a single board.

The faucet itself is real hardware — a pipe flange, PVC pipe, and elbow fittings — spray-painted to match, not a molded plastic look-alike. That's what makes the running water believable instead of gimmicky.

materials.name: Plywood or pine boards, assorted
materials.note: Top, front, back, and sides, sized to your chosen basin.

steps.title: Choose the basin and size the cabinet
steps.body: Pick the plastic container first — everything else in this build is measured off it, which is why there is no cut list. Set it upside down on your sheet stock and trace the rim; the cabinet top has to be at least that footprint plus enough margin behind the basin for the faucet flange to sit flat, and enough on each side that the top does not look skimpy. Set the cabinet height from the child who will use it rather than from the basin, so the rim lands at about their waist and they can reach into it without stretching. Write down the top's length and width and the cabinet height before cutting anything, because the front, back and sides all come off those three numbers.

steps.title: Cut the cabinet pieces
steps.body: Cut the top to the length and width you recorded, then the front and back to the top's length by the cabinet height, and the two sides to that same height by the top's depth. Decide now whether the front and back overlap the side edges or sit between them, and subtract two board thicknesses from whichever pair ends up captured — get this backwards and the finished cabinet is two board thicknesses off in one direction. Then mark the basin opening in the top: trace the container's rim, then draw a second line inside that one, set in by the width of the container's lip, so the basin drops through and hangs by its rim. Cut to the inner line and test-fit the basin before you assemble anything, because an opening cut slightly small can be filed out and one cut large cannot be put back.

steps.title: Build the cabinet
steps.body: Set the pocket-hole jig for the actual thickness of your stock and drill the ends of the front, back and sides, keeping every hole on an inside face where it will not show. Assemble in the order the plan gives — the top first, then the front and back to the top, then the sides — so the top stays the reference face and the basin opening ends up where you cut it. Check the box for square by measuring both diagonals across the top before the last screws go in; a racked cabinet will not stand flat and the basin will not seat level in its opening.

steps.title: Build the faucet
steps.body: Thread a short length of PVC pipe into the 1/2" pipe flange, put an elbow on top of it, and a second short piece out of the elbow to form the spout. The flange is the base — it screws flat to the cabinet top behind the basin — so make the upright tall enough that the elbow clears the basin rim, and the spout long enough that it actually overhangs the water. Dry-fit the whole assembly on the cabinet and hold it where it will live before you commit to any length. Spray paint the assembled faucet, letting it dry fully between coats, and paint the knob hardware at the same time so the whole set matches.

steps.title: Add the spinning knobs
steps.body: Drill a hole through the cabinet top on each side of the faucet, sized to the machine screw's shank so the screw turns freely in the wood rather than threading into it — that free turn is the entire trick, and a hole drilled tight kills it. Pass each screw up from underneath, run a nut on above the top, and cap the exposed end so the cap spins on the screw. Tighten the underside nut only enough to stop the screw dropping through, then test that both knobs turn easily before you move on.

steps.title: Install the pump
steps.body: Sit the submersible pump in the bottom of the basin and push one end of the vinyl tubing onto its outlet. Route the tubing out of the basin, down inside the cabinet, and back up through the PVC faucet so its free end sits just inside the spout — the 3 ft on the materials list is what makes that route possible, so lead it in gentle curves and keep it off tight bends, which will throttle the flow. Fill the basin, run the pump, and check for leaks at the spout and around the basin rim before you hand it over. Route the pump's cord out of the back and secure it where a child cannot pull on it.
